name: Jallao (SS-368)
events: This page dealing with the wartime history of USS Jallao was created in December 2025.
[No deck logs of this submarine are currently available to us so therefore some details, particularly regarding trials and exercises, might be missing on this page.]
10 Jul 1944 From 10 to 26 July 1944, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted trials and exercises on the Great Lakes. (1)
26 Jul 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) departed Manitowoc for Chicago. (1)
27 Jul 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) departed Chicago for New Orleans. Most part of this passage was made in a special dry dock. (1)
1 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) arrived at New Orleans from Chicago. (1)
6 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) departed New Orleans for the Panama Canal Zone.
Total bombing and attack restrictions were in place, 30 miles ahead, 60 miles behind and 15 miles on either side.
Escort was to be provided until point 'F' by USS SC-663 (Lt.(jg) T.A. Coffey, USNR). The remainder of the passage was to be made unescorted. (1)
11 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) arrived at Coco Solo, Panama Canal Zone where she was wiped (degaussed). (1)
12 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) transited the Panama Canal and arrived at Balboa. (1)
14 Aug 1944 From 14 August to 1 September 1944, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted a training period in the Gulf of Panama. (1)
16 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN).
Apparently also an exercise (possibly a practice attack) was carried out with (/ on) USS Detroit (T/Capt. D.C., Jr., USN).
17 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN).
18 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN).
19 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN).

20 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN). USS Mallard was however soon replaced by USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR).
21 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR).
22 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR).
23 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR).
24 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR).
26 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR) and USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN).
27 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR) and USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN).

28 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR), USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN), USS SC-654 (Lt.(jg) R. Engelman, USNR) and USS SC-661 (Lt.(jg). R.D. Holsclaw, USNR).
29 Aug 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR).
5 Sep 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) departed Balboa for Pearl Harbor. (1)
22 Sep 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) arrived at Pearl Harbor from Balboa, Panama Canal Zone.
Until 8 October 1944 another period of training exercises was carried out. (1)
27 Sep 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.

28 Sep 1944 USS Tambor (T/Cdr. W.J. Germershausen, Jr., USN) and USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Le Hardy (Lt.Cdr. E.L. Holtz, USNR), USS Tisdale (Lt.Cdr. J.S. Hatfield, USNR), USS Connolly (Lt.Cdr. W.A. Collier, USNR) and USS Widgeon (Lt. C.W. Engler, USN).
USS Tambor remained out for night exercises with USS Le Hardy and USS Fleming (Lt. K.F. Burgess, Jr., USNR).

29 Sep 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Atule (T/Cdr. J.H. Maurer, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Charles R. Greer (Cdr. A. Jackson, Jr., USNR) and USS Fleming (Lt. K.F. Burgess, Jr., USNR).
Later USS Haddock (T/Cdr. J.P. Roach, USN) also joined for night exercises.
1 Oct 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.

2 Oct 1944 USS Halibut (T/Cdr. I.J. Galantin, USN), USS Pogy (T/Lt.Cdr. P.G. Molteni, Jr., USN), USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Allen (Lt. J.A. Rowe, USNR), USS Le Hardy (Lt.Cdr. E.L. Holtz, USNR), USS Manlove (Lt. E.P. Foster, Jr., USNR), USS Connolly (Lt.Cdr. W.A. Collier, USNR) and USS Widgeon (Lt. C.W. Engler, USN).

3 Oct 1944 USS Tuna (T/Cdr. E.F. Steffanides, Jr., USN), USS Pogy (T/Lt.Cdr. P.G. Molteni, Jr., USN), USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Allen (Lt. J.A. Rowe, USNR), USS Le Hardy (Lt.Cdr. E.L. Holtz, USNR), USS Fleming (Lt. K.F. Burgess, Jr., USNR), USS Connolly (Lt.Cdr. W.A. Collier, USNR) and USS Widgeon (Lt. C.W. Engler, USN).

5 Oct 1944 During 5/6 October 1944, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Atule (T/Cdr. J.H. Maurer, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Connolly (Lt.Cdr. W.A. Collier, USNR) and USS Hemminger (Lt.Cdr. J.R. Bodler, USNR).
Later, USS Pintado (T/Cdr. B.A. Clarey, USN) and USS Charles R. Greer (Lt. A.G. Raht, USNR) also joined for exercises.

6 Oct 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Atule (T/Cdr. J.H. Maurer, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Charles R. Greer (Lt. A.G. Raht, USNR) and USS Connolly (Lt.Cdr. W.A. Collier, USNR).
9 Oct 1944 At 1330 W/9, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) departed from Pearl Harbor for her 1st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Philippines.

She was part of a coordinated attack group which was also made up of USS Pintado (T/Cdr. B.A. Clarey, USN) and USS Atule (T/Cdr. J.H. Maurer, USN). T/Cdr. Clarey was the group commander. They departed Pearl Harbor as a group in company of each other escorted by a PC-boat [not identified] until dark. They were to proceed to Saipan first before continuing to their patrol area. En-route to Saipan exercises were to be carried out.

21 Oct 1944 Around 1030 K/21, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN), USS Pintado (T/Cdr. B.A. Clarey, USN) and USS Atule (T/Cdr. J.H. Maurer, USN) arrived at Saipan from Pearl Harbor. They were escorted in by USS PGM-9 (Lt. C. Morgan, USNR) which had joined around 0445 K/21.
All three submarines were taken in hand for voyage repairs by USS Fulton (T/Capt. A.A. Clarkson, USN).

22 Oct 1944 Around 0915 I/22, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN), USS Pintado (T/Cdr. B.A. Clarey, USN) and USS Atule (T/Cdr. J.H. Maurer, USN) departed Saipan for their patrol area. They were escorted out by USS PGM-9 (Lt. C. Morgan, USNR) until 1530 I/22.

23 Oct 1944 At 0905 I/23, on board USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) a lookout sighted a periscope which was avoided on the surface by presenting stern on and going to flank speed. No position is given in the patrol report but about 2.5 hours USS Jallao was in position 16°45'N, 141°41'E. (1)

25 Oct 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) torpedoed and sank the already damaged Japanese light cruiser Tama (offsite link) east of Luzon Strait in position 21°23'N, 127°19'E. The entire crew of the cruiser was lost with their ship.
2004 I/25 - Obtained a radar contact at 27000 yards. Commenced tracking. Enemy course was 035° at 16 knots. Sent the information to the wolf pack.
2058 I/25 - USS Pintado (T/Cdr. B.A. Clarey, USN) joined. Moonlight was very bright. The enemy ship could be seen from 20000 yards range.

2242 I/25 - Dived to radar depth when the range was 12000 yards. It was thought the enemy was a battleship so the depth of the torpedoes was set to 15 feet. When the range had closed to 4000 yards the enemy was recognised as a Natori-class light cruiser through the periscope. When the range had been 20000 yards there were two radar pips on the screen but only the light cruiser could now be seen.

2301 I/25 - In position 21°23'N, 127°19'E, fired three torpedoes from the bow tubes from 1200 yards. A muffled explosion was heard about three minutes after firing followed by two end of run explosions. The light cruiser had turned towards with zero angle on the bow. USS Jallao swung left for a stern shot. Depth setting to the torpedoes was changed to 10 feet. The light cruiser altered course to the left and presented a good stern shot with a range of only 700 yards.

2305 I/25 - Fired four stern torpedoes. The first hit the exact center of the cruiser. A second hit was seen at about the after mast and a third hit was seen between the bow and the bridge. All hits were followed by a brilliant flash and much smoke. The cruiser was soon enveloped in a pall of black smoke. Jallao went to 80 feet and then to 150 feet and commenced to reload the torpedo tubes.

2328 I/25 - Surfaced. There was nothing in sight. Set course to rejoin USS Pintado and USS Atule (T/Cdr. J.H. Maurer, USN) which were to the northward and on track of the cruiser. USS Pintado signalled that she had seen the enemy break in half and sink. (2)

1 Nov 1944 At 0015 H/1, in the South-China Sea, south-east of the Pratas Islands, in position 19°48'N, 117°23'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) obtained a radar contact at 30000 yards on bearing 312°. The target tracked on a base course of 135° but when the range had closed to 18000 yards the target changed course to 090° and later to 045°. Information was sent to the wolf pack. USS Pintado (T/Cdr. B.A. Clarey, USN) was about 10000 yards astern of USS Jallao but had not yet made contact. It was however a loosing game. The target tracked at 17 knots while the best speed USS Jallao could do in the sea conditions was 14.5 knots.
At 0240 H/1, contact was lost at 32000 yards. (1)

3 Nov 1944 At 1900 H/3, in position 17°31'N, 117°34'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) obtained a radar contact at a range of 22000 yards. Commenced tracking and made a contact report to the wolf pack. Two large pips were on the radar screen. When the range had come down to 18000 yards, two more pips appeared on the radar screen. The enemy force tracked at a speed of 20 knots.

At 2155 H/3, a tremendous explosion was seen that shot flame and smoke a thousand feet into the sky. This was the work of USS Pintado (T/Cdr. B.A. Clarey, USN) which hit a Japanese destroyer with four torpedoes. One large contact was seen to head off to the north-east so decided to follow her.
At 2215 H/3, contact was lost with the ship that was retiring to the north-east. The range had been opening fast.
At 2305 H/3, the chase was broken off and course was set to return to the assigned patrol position.
The task force contacted was made up of the aircraft carrier Junyo and light cruiser Kiso with their destroyer escort made up of the Akikaze, Uzuki and Yuzuki (all offsite links). (2)

At 2155 H/3, a tremendous explosion was seen that shot flame and smoke a thousend feet into the sky. This was the work of USS Pintado (T/Cdr. B.A. Clarey, USN) which hit a Japanese destroyer with four torpedoes. One large contact was seen to head off to the north-east so decided to follow her.
13 Nov 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) attacked but missed the Japanese aircraft carrier Junyo with a full bow salvo of six torpedoes. All missed though.
Junyo was on passage from Manila to Mako together with the damaged heavy cruiser Tone. They were escorted by the destroyers Shigure, Uzuki and Yuzuki (all offsite links).

0402 I/13 - South-west of Formosa, obtained two radar contacts on bearing 211° at a range of 22000 yards. They tracked on a base course of 035° at 16 knots. Sent a contact report to the other wolfpack members.
0615 I/13 - Dived for a dawn attack.

0623 I/13 - The target group was identified as a Tone-class cruiser and a Hiyo-class aircraft carrier with three destroyers escorting. Two destroyer were on either bow of the carrier about 2000 yards ahead. The cruiser was just forward of the port beam of the carrier and one destroyer astern at 1000 yards. Started attack.

0640 I/13 - In position 21°02'N, 118°38'E, fired six bow torpedoes from 1400 yards at the carrier. Unfortunately the force changed course and all torpedoes missed. The carrier passed only 200 yards from the submarine after the course change.
0645 I/13 - End of run explosions were heard. Meanwhile USS Jallao had gone deep as the trailing destroyer was only about 500 yards ahead.

0647 I/13 - Five depth charged exploded but none were close. All three destroyers came back but only two of them stayed around to hunt the attacker. These stayed in the area until 0800 I/13 but never made contact. (2)
14 Nov 1944 At 1813 I/14, south of Formosa, in position 21°30'N, 121°05'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted smoke bearing 097°. It was however soon lost from sight.

At 1820 I/14, the smoke was seen again and also the masts of a ship were seen. Shortly afterwards the mast of two more medium sized transports were seen. No escorts could be seen at this moment but later it was seen that escort vessels were present. Changed course to close. The enemy was proceeding to the north.
At 1840 I/13 it was intended to surface but from that moment until two hours later USS Jallao was kept down by aircraft contacts.
At 2045 I/14, USS Jallao finally surfaced and went ahead full on course 330° and started an end around.
At 2145 I/14, USS Jallao had to dive again for radar equipped enemy aircraft surfacing again 15 minutes later.

At 2214 I/14, USS Jallao had to dive once more for enemy aircraft surfacing again almost an hour later. Contact with the convoy was now lost and it was estimated that it was now about 30 miles to the north. The chase was now broken off and course was changed to the south. (1)

18 Nov 1944 At 1820 I/18, west-south-west of Formosa, in position 21°23'N, 119°30'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted a patrol boat, PC-type size, at a range of only 2000 yards. The enemy was almost stationary. USS Jallao rigged for silent running and cleared the area at slow speed. (1)

25 Nov 1944 At 1210 I/25, north of Luzon, Philippines, in position 19°42'N, 121°40'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted the tops of a convoy bearing 105°. Enemy course was southerly. Range was about 15000 yards. An approach was commenced.

1230 I/25, USS Jallao went to battle stations submerged and tried to close the last ship in the column. The nearest USS Jallao was able to approach was about 9000 yards. The convoy was made up of one large and three medium transports. A destroyer was ahead and also one was astern as was a smaller escort. The convoy was also covered by two aircraft.
At 1332 I/25, USS Jallao secured from battle stations when the tops of the convoy only were visible.

At 1925 I/25, radar contact was obtained at a range of 10000 yards. These were only two small pips. Commenced tracking. Enemy course was north at 12 knots. When the range had closed to about 8000 yards one of them could be seen. It was a patrol vessel about the size of a PC-class.

At 1957 I/25, the enemy changed course towards and kept on a steady course. The leading patrol boat showed a white light in the direction of USS Jallao on five separate occasions. Speed was increased to flank and USS Jallao cleared the area to the north of Balintang. It was intended to round Balintang Island and re-enter the patrol area by going through the channel to the southwards of Bubuyan Island. (1)

26 Nov 1944 At 0520 I/26, south-west of Balintang Island, in position 19°50'N, 122°00'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) obtained a radar contact at a range of 7000 yards. Assumed this to be the Japanese patrol that usually hangs out to the north-west of Babuyan Island. Avoided it at flank speed. Contact was lost at a range of 9000 yards.

At 1119 I/26, while patrolling submerged, west of Balintang Island in position 19°55'N, 121°46'E, smoke and two masts of a large transport were sighted. Also a single mast of an escort was sighted. Contact was bearing 094° at a range of 16000 yards. Enemy course was southerly. It was not possible to close to attacking range. The patrol boat contacted earlier that morning was seen to heading towards.
At 1206 I/26, USS Jallao went to 100 feet to let the patrol vessel pass astern.
At 1855 I/26, USS Jallao surfaced.
At 2004 I/26, south-west of Sabtang Island, in position 20°00'N, 121°41'E, sighted a patrol vessel at a range of 7000 yards. It was avoided at flank speed on the surface.
At 2311 I/26, in position 20°04'N, 121°42'E, obtained radar contact on a patrol vessel at a range of 8000 yards which was avoided. Contact was lost at 9000 yards. (1)
27 Nov 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) left patrol to proceed to Majuro. (1)

10 Dec 1944 Around 1345 M/10, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) ended her 1st war patrol at Majuro. She was escorted in by USS Ramsay (Lt. R.R. Pollard, Jr., USNR) which had joined around 0945 M/10.
She was taken in hand for refit by USS USS Howard W. Gilmore (T/Capt. D.N. Cone, Jr., USN). Refit was completed on 25 December 1944. (3)

27 Dec 1944 From 27 December 1944 to 3 January 1945, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted a training programme off Majuro. Sometimes individually and sometimes as member of a coordinated attack group. (4)
27 Dec 1944 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted independent exercises off Majuro during which she was escorted by USS Ramsay (Lt. R.R. Pollard, Jr., USNR).

31 Dec 1944 While exercising off Majuro, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) reported sighting a possible periscope which was considered to be from an enemy submarine. USS Ramsay (Lt. R.R. Pollard, Jr., USNR) and USS Brackett (Lt.Cdr. A.C. Reed, Jr., USNR) searched the area which continued until the following day. No enemy submarine was detected though. (5)

31 Dec 1944 USS Sargo (T/Lt.Cdr. J.M. Hingson, USN), USS Pompon (T/Cdr. S.H. Gimber, USN), USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN), USS Atule (T/Cdr. J.H. Maurer, USN) and USS Spadefish (T/Cdr. G.W. Underwood, USN) conducted exercises off Majuro with USS Ramsay (Lt. R.R. Pollard, Jr., USNR) and USS Florikan (Lt. D. Hima, USN).
The exercises were broken off when USS Jallao reported seeing a periscope which was thought to be from an enemy submarine.

1 Jan 1945 During 1 and 2 January USS Pompon (T/Cdr. S.H. Gimber, USN), USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN), USS Atule (T/Cdr. J.H. Maurer, USN) and USS Spadefish (T/Cdr. G.W. Underwood, USN) conducted exercises off Majuro with USS Ramsay (Lt. R.R. Pollard, Jr., USNR) and USS Florikan (Lt. D. Hima, USN). (6)
3 Jan 1945 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) is docked at Majuro in ARD-18 to change the port propeller. She was undocked either later the same day or the following day. (4)
6 Jan 1945 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) departed from Majuro for her 2nd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Yellow Sea.

6 Jan 1945 Around 0930 M/6, USS Pompon (T/Cdr. S.H. Gimber, USN), USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN), USS Atule (T/Cdr. J.H. Maurer, USN) and USS Spadefish (T/Cdr. G.W. Underwood, USN) got underway from Majuro for their 7th, 2nd, 2nd and 3rd war patrols respectively. The submarines were to proceed to Saipan first to top off with fuel and provisions before proceeding to their patrol area in the Yellow Sea. They were escorted out until around 1420 M/6, by USS Ramsay (Lt. R.R. Pollard, Jr., USNR).
At 1615 M/6, USS Spadefish was mistaken for an enemy submarine by two friendly fighter aircraft and bombed after having dived. Some minor damage was caused. (7)

14 Jan 1945 Around 1130 K/14, USS Pompon (T/Cdr. S.H. Gimber, USN), USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN), USS Atule (T/Cdr. J.H. Maurer, USN) and USS Spadefish (T/Cdr. G.W. Underwood, USN) arrived at Saipan from Majuro. They were escorted in by USS PC-1126 (Lt. J.F Carrere, USNR) which had joined around 0530 K/14.

At Saipan all four submarines were taken in hand for voyage repairs by USS Fulton (T/Capt. A.A. Clarkson, USN). En-route to Saipan, USS Jallao had received permission to have her bow planes modified which would take several days to accomplish. She was thus unable to depart the following day with the coordinated attack group and was replaced. (8)
24 Jan 1945 From 24 to 26 January 1945 USS Sunfish (T/Cdr. E.E. Shelby, USN) and USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises off Saipan. (4)
28 Jan 1945 At 1015 K/28, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) departed Saipan for her patrol area.
She is to form a coordinated attack group with USS Gato (T/Cdr. R.M. Farrell, USN) and USS Sunfish (T/Cdr. E.E. Shelby, USN) which would departed the following day. (4)
1 Feb 1945 At 1945 I/1, south of Kyushu, Japan, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Parche (T/Lt.Cdr. W.W. McCrory, USN) exchanged recognition signals. (9)

1 Feb 1945 At 2130 I/1, south of Yakushima, in position 29°45'N, 130°36'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) obtained a possible radar contact bearing 327° at a range of 19800 yards. The tracking party soon confirmed it to be a ship contact. USS Jallao closed to 14000 yards an at ...

2145 I/1, the target now showed an escort or smaller vessel abeam, about 335° and at range of 14000 yards. The original contact was now bearing 000° at a range of 10500 yards. The range was closing rapidly. Considered this new contact may be a US submarine and tried to make contact. There was a lot of radar interference. When the range had come down to 6000 yards the contact turned to a course of 200° and opened the range at full speed. The contact later tracked at 15 knots on a course between 160° to 170°. USS Jallao took avoiding action to the southwest and west. The original contact had meanwhile been lost. It was later regained at a range of 26000 yards when course was altered back to the north. An end around was not possible due to the proximity of land so the chase was abandoned. (4)
2 Feb 1945 At 0555 I/1, south of the Danjo Islands, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Gato (T/Cdr. R.M. Farrell, USN) exchanged recognition signals. (4)
3 Feb 1945 At 0800 K/3, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) entered her patrol area. (4)

6 Feb 1945 At 1811 I/6, while patrolling submerged south of Saishu Island [now called Jeju Island] in position 32°35'N, 126°17'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted a patrol vessel bearing 073° at a range of 6000 yards. (4)

7 Feb 1945 At 2227 I/7, south of Saishu Island [now called Jeju Island] in position 32°36'N, 126°41'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) obtained a radar contact at a range of 5000 yards. It was identified as a small wooden patrol vessel which was avoided. Contact was lost at 5500 yards. (4)

9 Feb 1945 At 0823 I/9, while patrolling submerged south of Saishu Island [now called Jeju Island] in position 32°35'N, 126°27'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted a small fishing boat bearing 311° at a range of 4000 to 5000 yards. (4)

10 Feb 1945 At 0823 I/9, south of Saishu Island [now called Jeju Island] in position 32°26'N, 126°29'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) obtained radar contact on a small boat, possibly a patrol vessel, at a range of 4500 yards. It was avoided on the surface. Radar contact was lost at 5000 yards.

At 2204 I/9, south-south-west of Saishu Island in position 32°40'N, 126°02'E, radar contact was obtained at a range of 5000 yards. The small vessel was not identified but was avoided on the surface. (4)
12 Feb 1945 At 0155 I/12, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Gato (T/Cdr. R.M. Farrell, USN) exchanged recognition signals. (4)
13 Feb 1945 At 1855 I/13, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Gato (T/Cdr. R.M. Farrell, USN) exchanged recognition signals. (10)

14 Feb 1945 At 0903 I/14, south of Saishu Island [now called Jeju Island] in position 32°38'N, 126°18'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted two sampans thought to be patrol vessels. Range was 7000 yards.

At 0932 I/14, they apparently sighted the periscope as they suddenly turned towards and closed at high speed. USS Jallao went to 150 feet and took evasive action. They remained near the submarine until noon.
In the evening an attack by USS Gato (T/Cdr. R.M. Farrell, USN) was witnessed. (4)

16 Feb 1945 At 0849 I/16, off the Chinese coast to the south-east of Shanghai, in position 29°30'N, 122°41'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted a small two-masted sailing vessel (junk) proceeding on a course of 180°. Range was 8000 yards.

At 1700 I/16, in position 29°49'N, 122°14'E [doubtful if this position mentioned in the patrol report is correct, seems to be too far inshore], two small sailing vessels were seen. 15 minutes later a patrol vessel was also seen. (4)

17 Feb 1945 At 1104 I/17, in position 32°10'N, 124°22'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted what was thought to be an enemy patrol vessel dead ahead with zero angle on the bow. The high lookouts also saw it.

At 1107 I/17, USS Jallao dived. It was considered the contact may have been USS Sunfish (T/Cdr. E.E. Shelby, USN) which was most likely correct as she was in more or less the same position at this time having had to dive about two hours before for an aircraft contact.
At 2201 I/17, USS Jallao and USS Sunfish exchanged recognition signals with each other. (11)
20 Feb 1945 At 0225 I/20, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Gato (T/Cdr. R.M. Farrell, USN) exchanged recognition signals. (12)
20 Feb 1945 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) attacked a single transport with a total of six torpedoes south-east of Qingdao, China in position 35°24'N, 125°23'E. No hits were obtained.
0535 I/20 - In position 34°52'N, 124°01'E, obtained radar contact at a range of 19000 yards. Tracking was commenced.

0632 I/20 - Dived in what was hoped to be a position ahead of the target. Course of the target was determined to be about 290° at a speed of 8 knots. Range was closed to 12000 yards. No escorts or aircraft were seen. The target was a single freighter.
1025 I/20 - Surfaced to make an end around. Kept the smoke in sight.
1243 I/20 - Two aircraft, either Zekes or Rufes, were sighted at a range of 12 miles. They turned towards so at 1246 I/20, USS Jallao dived.
1435 I/20 - The smoke of the target could still be sighted. Due to the slow speed of the target it was considered still possible to catch him before he reached shallow waters.
1604 I/20 - Surfaced and continued the end around.
1807 I/20 - In position 35°25'N, 121°46'E, a Chinese junk was seen bearing 219° at a range of about 6 miles.
2045 I/20 - Radar contact was made on the target. Commenced tracking. It was a brilliant moonlight night with glassy sea and no swell.
2141 I/20, Dived when the range was 12000 yards to complete the attack submerged.
2218 I/20 - In position 35°49'N, 121°33'E, fired four torpedoes from yards. No hits were obtained.
2216 I/20 - Fired two stern tubes. Again no hits were obtained. All torpedoes fired so far had been heard on the sound gear to be running normal.
2336 I/20 - Surfaced and returned to our patrol position. (4)

21 Feb 1945 At 0630 I/21, when about to dive in position 35°42'N, 123°05'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN), obtained radar contact on bearing 080° at a range of 15000 yards. A small ship was sighted ahead.
At 0637 I/21, USS Jallao dived when the range was 12000 yards. Commenced approach.
At 0703 I/21, USS Jallao went to battle stations submerged. Range was now about 8000 yards and it was seen to be a patrol vessel of the converted whale catcher type. The enemy was now heading away.
At 0727 I/21, USS Jallao secured from battle stations. She conducted a dived patrol until surfacing at 1915 I/21. (4)
23 Feb 1945 At 1747 I/23, in position 36°55'N, 123°42'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN), sighted a small fishing sampan bearing 330° at a range of 5000 yards. (4)

27 Feb 1945 At 0540 I/27, in position 35°18'N, 124°07'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) obtained radar contact on a ship at a range of 15000 yards. The range was slightly closed and tracking was commenced which showed a course of about 060° at a speed of 10 to 12 knots. The range was opened to 22000 yards, keeping the target in sight with high periscope. It was decided to make a daylight end around as it was not possible to close the track before daylight. Radar contact was lost at 18000 yards. At 12000 yards three more small pips had been sighted on the radar screen. The largest target looked like one of the modern escort vessels (frigates / kaibokan) but this was not sure. The end around progressed satisfactory until ...

0749 I/27, an aircraft contact was obtained at a range of 6000 yards and one minute later a Betty was seen dropping out of low clouds two miles away coming straight towards. USS Jallao crash dived. The end around now had to be abandoned and USS Jallao remained submerged until 1925 I/27. (4)
2 Mar 1945 At 0905 I/2, in position 34°15'N, 123°52'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted a Chinese junk bearing 125° at a range of 7000 yards. (4)

4 Mar 1945 At 1751 I/4, in position 34°27'N, 123°15'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted the tops of three ships bearing 203° at a range of 12000 to 14000 yards. They were on a westerly course. As it was not possible to close for a submerged attack USS Jallao surfaced at 1930 I/4 and commenced an end around for a night attack.
At 2010 I/4, radar range was 18000 yards. Commenced tracking. A medium size tanker and a medium size transport were accompanied by two destroyers or frigates.

At 2306 I/4, USS Jallao dived and commenced an approach at radar depth but the attack had to be broken off due to problems with the TDC (Torpedo Data Computer) which resulted in an inaccurate setup. It was decided to make another end around while attempting to make repairs to the TDC. (4)
5 Mar 1945 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) attacked a convoy made up of a medium sized tanker and medium sized transport and two large escorts. Two torpedoes were fired but both missed.
0042 I/5 - Surfaced and started a new end around on the convoy first detected at 1751 I/4 (see the event for 4 March 1945).

0303 I/5, Dived to radar depth. Commenced approach. The escort on on e side was approximately 1000 yards abeam of one of the targets. Closed while at radar depth to 3000 yards from the escort. Range to the target was 3500 yards. Went to periscope depth. Visibility through the periscope was good. When the range to the target was 2000 yards, the periscope was swung to check the near escort which had been drawing aft on sound bearings, except for the last three bearing called out. When T/Cdr. Icenhower had turned the periscope 30° he suddenly ordered down periscope and right full rudder. However No.1 periscope was hit by the propeller of the escort. The attack was continued with No.2 periscope. Sound could not pick up the escort so it was thought he was stopped. Setup a new attack and at ...

0351 I/5 - In position 34°53'N, 124°51'E, fired two stern torpedoes from 1000 yards. The torpedoes tracked normal by sound but no hits were obtained. No end of run explosions were heard. The TDC (Torpedo Data Computer) was still not working properly.
0433 I/5 - Surfaced. It was not possible to make another end around on this convoy due to the proximity of land.
0645 I/5 - Dived. Commenced work on repairing the TDC. This was completed at 1630 I/5. (4)

6 Mar 1945 At 0240 I/6, in the Yellow Sea [no position given in the patrol report], USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted the white light of a supposed fishing vessel bearing 240° at a range of 5600 yards. Contact was lost at 7000 yards. (4)

7 Mar 1945 At 0934 I/7, in the Yellow Sea, in position 34°50'N, 122°34'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted a Chinese junk bearing 070° at a range of 6000 yards. She was heading to the north-west.

At 1850 I/7, in position 34°25'N, 123°00'E, two patrol boats (sampan type) were sighted bearing 220° at a range of 7000 yards. They were of the same type that had been encountered on 14 February 1945 to the south of Saishu Island. (4)
9 Mar 1945 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) attacked a Japanese convoy to the north-west of Amami-o-Shima in position 29°04'N, 128°28'E. Six torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained.

This was convoy Kana 803 which was made up of the transports Dokan Maru (2270 GRT, built 1944), Keizan Maru (2308 GRT, built 1918) and Sanka Maru (2488 GRT, built 1918). It was on passage from Kagoshima, Kyushu, Japan to Naha, Okinawa and was escorted by the frigates Kaibokan 44, Kaibokan 118, auxiliary minesweeper Taihei Maru No.3 (197 GRT, built 1930) and auxiliary netlayer Shinto Maru No.2 (540 GRT, built 1944) (all links are offsite links).
It was Kaibokan 44 which depth charged USS Jallao at 2345 I/9.
All three transports were sunk the following day by USS Kete (T/Lt.Cdr. E. Ackerman, USN).

1752 I/9 - In the East China Sea, east of Yakushima, in position 30°29'N, 128°52'E, while dived conducting periscope patrol, the tops of three ships were sighted on bearing 153°. Headed towards and picked up one frigate and one submarine chaser with the targets which looked like three medium sized transports.

1920 I/9 - Surfaced. A contact report was sent to all boats in the area but only USS Segundo (T/Cdr. J.D. Fulp, Jr., USN) replied. Tracking had showed the course of the enemy as being 215° at 7.5 knots. A third escort was also seen on the radar screen.
2000 I/9 - Recognition signals and info were exchanged with the SJ radar with USS Kete who told USS Jallao that they would attack after her.
2313 I/9 - Dived to radar depth and commenced approach.
2338 I/9 - In position 29°04'N, 128°28'E (north-west of Amami-o-Shima), fired six bow torpedoes at the overlapping transports from 2490 yards.
2343 I/9 - Heard an explosion which was hoped to be a hit on a target.

2345 I/9 - Heard the first of 17 depth charges in three groups. One escort passed down the starboard side and dropped seven depth charges about one second apart. All three escorts came back and hunted the attacker until 0030 I/9 when only one escort was hunting.

0045 I/10 - HE was no longer heard. Maybee the escort had stopped as his screws faded out very quickly. Radar interference was picked up on the convoy's port bow and it was assumed USS Kete was making her attack run but no sound of her making an attack was picked up.

0235 I/10 - Surfaced after having reloaded the torpedo tubes. The seas were very rough. Headed down the base course of the convoy. A large quantity of water entered the main induction. As the convoy was now about 25 miles away and the likelihood of completing an end around before dawn the case was quickly given up. Informed USS Balao (T/Lt.Cdr. R.K.R. Worthington, USN), which was to the south-west of USS Jallao of the bearing and distance of the convoy. Also informed her that USS Kete was also in contact with the convoy.

0403 I/10 - Sighted the red glow of a probable explosion on bearing 220° which was the general bearing of the convoy. USS Kete was asked USS Jallao did any damage. She replied that no damage had been done by USS Jallao.
0610 I/10 - Submerged and ran towards Gajashima which was sighted at 1829 I/10. USS Jallao then left patrol by transiting the Nansei Shoto to the south of Suwanosejima. (4)

12 Mar 1945 At 1243 I/12, in position 28°14'N, 137°25'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted a US submarine on the horizon. Shortly afterwards recognition signals were exchanged by SJ radar with USS Spot (T/Cdr. W.S. Post, Jr., USN) which had sighted USS Jallao at 1230 I/12. (13)
13 Mar 1945 At 2101 I/13, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) exchanged recognition signals by SJ radar with USS Ronquil (T/Lt.Cdr. R.B. Lander, USN). (14)
16 Mar 1945 Around 1230 I/16, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) arrived at Saipan. She was taken in hand for voyage repairs by USS Fulton (T/Capt. A.A. Clarkson, USN).
She was escorted in [identity of the escort not known]. The escort had joined shortly after 0500 I/16. (4)
17 Mar 1945 Around 1445 K/17, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) departed Saipan for Midway. She was escorted out until 2200 K/17 by USS LCI(L)-1054 (Lt.(jg) W.H. Spicer, USNR). (4)
26 Mar 1945 Around 1000 Y/26, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) ended her 2nd war patrol at Midway. She was taken in hand for refit by the submarine base and a relief crew.
11 Apr 1945 From 11 to 19 April 1945, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) had a four day training and followed by a loading period. The training period included one wolf pack exercise. (15)
20 Apr 1945 Around 1415 Y/20, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) departed from Midway for her 3rd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in Japanese home waters. (15)

28 Apr 1945 At 0420 K/28, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) received a signal from ComSubsPac ordering her to perform lifeguard duties off Marcus Island. Course was set to comply with these orders. (15)
30 Apr 1945 At 1146 K/30, in approximate position 23°53'N, 153°55'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted Marcus Island bearing 009° at a range of 13 miles.
Between 1422 K/30 and 1505 K/30 bomb explosions were seen on Marcus Island. (15)
2 May 1945 Between 1534 K/2 and 1540 K/2, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted bomb explosions on Marcus Island. (15)
7 May 1945 Around 1600 K/7, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted bomb explosions were seen on Marcus Island. Also anti-aircraft fire was seen coming from the Island. (15)
8 May 1945 Between 1248 K/8 and 1420 K/8, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted bomb explosions were seen on Marcus Island. Also anti-aircraft fire was seen coming from the Island.
At 1555 K/8, six large unidentified aircraft were seen approaching Marcus Island from the west. They landed about 10 minutes later so must have been Japanese. (15)

9 May 1945 At 1007 K/9, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) was informed that four life rafts had been sighted 5 miles to the north of Marcus Island. Speed was increased to flank and course was altered to proceed to the position reported.
At 1425 K/9, rescue operations were commenced. A total of five USN aviators of VPB 108 were picked up. Two of them were seriously wounded.

At 1440 K/9, while the rescue operations were still ongoing, enemy shore batteries on Marcus Island opened fire and shells landed fairly close but rescue operations were continued until they were completed three minutes later. USS Jallao then retired and a signal was sent reporting the situation and the state of the wounded.
At 0315 K/10, orders were received to proceed to Saipan. Course was set accordingly. (15)

11 May 1945 At 0910 K/11, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN), which was on passage to Saipan, exchanged recognition signals with USS Bang (T/Lt.Cdr. O.W. Bagby, Jr., USN) which was on passage from Saipan to Pearl Harbor.
At 1229 K/11, USS Jallao exchanged recognition signals with USS Bashaw (Lt.Cdr. H.S. Simpson, USNR) which was on passage from Guam to Pearl Harbor. (15)

11 May 1945 At 1258 K/11, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) exchanged recognition signals with USS Gilmore (Lt. T.F. Meyer, Jr., USNR). At 1330 K/11, rendezvous was made and a medical officer and rating were transferred by the destroyer escort to the submarine. The two ships remained in company during the passage to Saipan. (15)
12 May 1945 Around 1330 K/12, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Gilmore (Lt. T.F. Meyer, Jr., USNR) arrived at Saipan. (15)
13 May 1945 Around 1415 K/13, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) left Saipan to resume her 3rd war patrol. She was now to patrol in Japanese home waters.
She was escorted out until 1900 K/13 [Escort not identified in the patrol report]. (15)

16 May 1945 At 1220 K/16, in approximate position 22°56'N, 137°39'E, the northbound USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted and exchanged recognition signals with with the southbound USS Springer (T/Cdr. R. Kefauver, USN) which was en-route to Guam from patrol. (15)

17 May 1945 At 2153 K/17, the northbound USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted and exchanged recognition signals with with the southbound USS Trepang (T/Cdr. A.R. Faust, USN) which was en-route to Guam from patrol. (15)
18 May 1945 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) arrived in her assigned (temporary) lifeguard station in the vicinity of Sofu-Gan [also known as Lot's Wife (offsite link)]. (15)
19 May 1945 At 0515 K/19, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted a ship on the horizon to the west. Five minutes later Jallao dived.

At 0542 K/19, USS Jallao surfaced after the 'ship' had been identified as a US submarine which turned out to be USS Atule (T/Cdr. J.H. Maurer, USN) which was en-route from patrol to Pearl Harbor via Midway. (15)
20 May 1945 At 0155 K/20, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) left her temporary lifeguard station (now off Tori Shima) and headed for a new assigned patrol station off the Kii Suido.

At 1125 K/20, new orders were received and course was set to a temporary patrol station to the south-east of the Bungo Suido for an upcoming B-29 raid. Course was set to proceed to the position at flank speed. (15)
21 May 1945 Around 0200 I/21, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) arrived in her newly assigned temporary lifeguard station. Her services were however not required. (15)
22 May 1945 At 0243 I/21, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) exchanged recognition signals with USS Rasher (T/Lt.Cdr. C.D. Nace, USN) which was en-route to Midway after having left patrol. (15)
31 May 1945 At 2330 I/31, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) received new instructions to proceed to a new lifeguard station for an upcoming air raid on Osaka. Course was set accordingly. (15)
2 Jun 1945 At 0300 I/2, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) left her lifeguard station in accordance with orders to leave patrol and proceed to Pearl Harbor.
At 0537 I/2, recognition signals were exchanged with her relief, USS Peto (T/Cdr. R.H. Caldwell, Jr., USN).
At 1035 I/2, USS Jallao exchanged recognition signals with USS Pipefish (T/Cdr. W.N. Deragon, USN). (15)

10 Jun 1945 At 1535 Y/10, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) joined USS Plaice (T/Cdr. R.S. Andrews, USN) and USS Piper (T/Cdr. B.F. McMahon, USN) which were also en-route to Pearl Harbor returning from patrol in the Kuriles area. They then proceeded in company. (15)

13 Jun 1945 Around 1000 V/13, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN), USS Plaice (T/Cdr. R.S. Andrews, USN) and USS Piper (T/Cdr. B.F. McMahon, USN) ended their third, fifth and second war patrols at Pearl Harbor.
They were escorted in by USS PC-580 (Lt. A.L. Anderson, USNR) which had joined around 0500 V/13.
5 Jul 1945 During the period, 5 to 14 July, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted 9 days of training out of Pearl Harbor. (16)
5 Jul 1945 During 5/6 July 1945, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS J. Douglas Blackwood (Lt.Cdr. J.L. Johnston, USNR).
On the 6th, USS PC-817 (Lt. H.R. Stern, Jr., USNR) also joined the exercises.
6 Jul 1945 During the night of 6/7 July 1945, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises with USS Pruitt (Lt. D.B. Harby, USNR) and USS Howard (Lt.Cdr. F.W. Diehl, USNR).

7 Jul 1945 USS Catfish (Lt.Cdr. W.A. Overton, USNR), USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Searaven (T/Lt.Cdr. R.C. Smallwood, Jr., USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor together with USS Coolbaugh (Lt.Cdr. S.E. Zimmerman, USNR). (17)
8 Jul 1945 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Howard (Lt.Cdr. F.W. Diehl, USNR) and USS Florikan (T/Lt. D. Hima, USN).
9 Jul 1945 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.
12 Jul 1945 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.
13 Jul 1945 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.
16 Jul 1945 Around 1335 V/16 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) departed Pearl Harbor for her 4th war patrol. Before proceeding to her patrol area she was to proceed to Guam. (16)
23 Jul 1945 At 2154 M/23, north of Wake Island, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) exchanged recognition signals with USS Queenfish (T/Cdr. F.N. Shamer, USN) which was en-route to Midway. (16)
25 Jul 1945 At 1810 L/25, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) exchanged recognition signals with USS Sea Fox (T/Cdr. R.C. Klinker, USN) which was en-route to Midway. (16)
28 Jul 1945 Around 0730 K/28, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) made rendezvous with USS Threadfin (T/Cdr. J.J. Foote, USN) and her escort, USS SC 1041 (Lt.(jg) A.J. Hicks, Jr., USNR).
The escort had joined USS Threadfin about an hour earlier.
They arrived at Guam around 1030 K/28. USS Threadfin ending her 3rd war patrol. (18)

29 Jul 1945 USS Pargo (T/Cdr. D.B. Bell, USN), USS Devilfish (T/Lt.Cdr. S.S. Mann, Jr., USN), USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Tigrone (T/Lt.Cdr. V.E. Schumacher, USN) conducted exercises off Guam with USS Greenlet (T/Lt. R.B. Willhoite, USN). (16)
30 Jul 1945 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) conducted exercises off Guam with USS Greenlet (T/Lt. R.B. Willhoite, USN).
31 Jul 1945 Around 1700 K/31, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) departed from Guam for her patrol area. She was ordered to patrol in the Sea of Japan. (16)
3 Aug 1945 At 1847 K/25, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) exchanged recognition signals with USS Gabilan (T/Cdr. W.B. Parham, USN) which was en-route to Saipan from patrol.
USS Gabilan gives the time as 1828 K/25 and the position as 26°41'N, 139°45'E (well to the west of Chichi Jima). (19)
7 Aug 1945 At 2135 K/7, west of Fukue Island, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) exchanged recognition signals with USS Pomfret (T/Cdr. J.B. Hess, USN). (16)
8 Aug 1945 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) made the transit of the Tsushima Straits submerged and entered the Sea of Japan. (16)

11 Aug 1945 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) torpedoed and sank the Japanese merchant cargo ship Teihoku Maru (5794 GRT, built 1936, former French Pierre L.D., later renamed Persee) [offsite link] in the Sea of Japan in position 38°03'N, 133°12'E.
2140 K/10 - In position 38°28'N, 132°53'E, obtained SJ radar contact on a ship bearing 285° at a range of 17000 yards. Commenced tracking.

2302 K/10 - Having established the ships base course and speed, commenced to close to 6800 yards at which range the target was seen to be a very long, single-stacked ship which was low in the water. She was making for a Japanese port. Proceeded to gain a position ahead to attack.
0112 K/11 - After having gained a position ahead dived to complete the attack.
0145 K/11 - In position 38°03'N, 133°12'E fired four bow torpedoes from 1670 yards. Two hits were obtained.
0149 K/11 - The targets screws, which had stopped after the hits were heard to start up again so fired two more torpedoes from the stern tubes from 1000 yards. Both missed.
0213 K/11 - The target was listing but was still circling. Fired two more (bow) torpedoes from 1000 yards. One hit exactly amidships.
0218 K/11 - A tremendous explosion occurred in the target which sank one minute later. (20)

13 Aug 1945 At 0739 K/13, in position 39°57'N, 139°30'E [39°57'N, 134°30'E as given in the patrol report must be wrong], USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted two patrol vessels through the periscope, one off the northern end of the peninsula and one about four miles to the south of the first. Both were about three miles from the shore. A small coastal freighter was also seen, hugging the coast on a northerly course. Range to the freighter was 12000 yards.

Course was changed to the north to avoid the patrol vessels. One of the patrol vessels was heard to be pinging. While observing the northernmost patrol vessel she was seen to be signalling in the direction of USS Jallao. The range was 8000 yards at that moment. T/Cdr. Icenhower looked on the reverse of the bearing of the enemy patrol vessel but nothing could be sighted. The enemy did not close the range. (16)

15 Aug 1945 At 1045 K/15, in position 40°45'N, 139°27'E [41°45'N, 138°27'E as given in the patrol report must be wrong], USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) sighted a small junk at a range of 8000 to 10000 yards.

At 1210 K/15, while in position 40°25'N, 139°47'E, smoke was sighted bearing 073°. Course was altered to the north to intercept as it appeared the smoke indicated a ship that was proceeding across the Sea of Japan.
At 1422 K/15, the smoke was lost from sight.

At 1610 K/15, smoke was sighted bearing 178°. Course was changed to intercept. It appeared the source of this smoke was proceeding along the coast close in. The first source of smoke had drawn USS Jallao away from the coast and an interception was not possible. T/Cdr. Icenhower decided to remain close to the coast during the night to see if any traffic would be running during the night.
At 1824 K/15, smoke was sighted bearing 126°. The source of this smoke was proceeding northwards along the coast at 8 knots. USS Jallao was still too far from the coast to be able to intercept.
At 2130 K/15, a signal was received ordering the cessation of hostilities against Japan. (16)
16 Aug 1945 From 16 to 28 August 1945, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) remained in her patrol area. (16)

18 Aug 1945 At 0657 K/18, in position 41°48'N, 138°17'E, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) in light fog sighted a small ship bearing 065° proceeding on course 190° at 12 knots. The ship could not be identified. (16)

29 Aug 1945 Around 1700 K/29, in the Sea of Japan, well to the west of the northern end off Honshu, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Piper (T/Lt.Cdr. E.L. Beach, Jr., USN) made rendezvous. Some movies and phonographic records were exchanged. (21)

1 Sep 1945 Around 2020 K/29, in the Sea of Japan, well to the west of the northern end off Honshu, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Piper (T/Lt.Cdr. E.L. Beach, Jr., USN) made rendezvous. Both had received orders to leave patrol. They now proceeded southwards to leave patrol and make another rendezvous with (an)other submarine or submarines leaving patrol. (21)

3 Sep 1945 At 0815 K/3, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Piper (T/Lt.Cdr. E.L. Beach, Jr., USN) made rendezvous with USS Stickleback (T/Cdr. H.K. Nauman, USN) in approximate position 35°26'N, 129°53'E. Course was set to transit the Nishi Suido on the surface and proceed to Guam. (22)

9 Sep 1945 Around 0700 K/9, USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN), USS Piper (T/Lt.Cdr. E.L. Beach, Jr., USN) and USS Stickleback (T/Cdr. H.K. Nauman, USN) were joined by USS Torsk (T/Cdr. B.E. Lewellen, USN).
Shortly afterwards the four submarines made rendezvous with their escort to Guam, USS Steele (Lt. F. Seymour, USNR) which already had USS Pargo (T/Cdr. D.B. Bell, USN) in company.
At submarines arrived at Guam around 1100 K/9 ending their last war patrols. All were taken in hand for voyage repairs by USS Sperry (T/Capt. R.J. Ramsbotham, USN). (23)

10 Sep 1945 USS Pargo (T/Cdr. D.B. Bell, USN), USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN), USS Piper (T/Lt.Cdr. E.L. Beach, Jr., USN), USS Stickleback (T/Cdr. H.K. Nauman, USN) and USS Torsk (T/Cdr. B.E. Lewellen, USN) departed Guam for Pearl Harbor.

19 Sep 1945 USS Pargo (T/Cdr. D.B. Bell, USN), USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN), USS Piper (T/Lt.Cdr. E.L. Beach, Jr., USN), USS Stickleback (T/Cdr. H.K. Nauman, USN) and USS Torsk (T/Cdr. B.E. Lewellen, USN) arrived at Pearl Harbor from Guam.
22 Sep 1945 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Stickleback (T/Cdr. H.K. Nauman, USN) departed Pearl Harbor for San Francisco.
28 Sep 1945 USS Jallao (T/Cdr. J.B. Icenhower, USN) and USS Stickleback (T/Cdr. H.K. Nauman, USN) arrived at San Francisco from Pearl Harbor.
